entry.title: Error in ADT-Plugin 3.60 when creating a new Data Definition
entry.content:
I encounter a NullPointerException in ADT-Plugin 3.60 when trying to create a new Data Definition by right-clicking on a package node and selecting 'New Data Definition'.

The error occurs because adtObject is null if no ABAP object is selected, only a structural package node.

As a work-around it is possible to "right-click" on an existing Data Definition

entry.title: SAP Business Network will support regulatory consistent processing of e-Invoices in Spain
entry.content:
Europe’s regulatory landscape is shifting, with countries steadily introducing e-invoicing mandates to tackle the VAT gap. An additional country to add to this digital transformation is Spain. 

The Spanish e-invoicing mandate will be rolled out in different phases, starting 12 months after the publication of the ministerial order, which has been announced for October 2026. At that time, companies with an annual turnover exceeding 8 million euros will have to start issuing and receiving invoices, and notify corresponding statuses.  Companies with an annual turnover of less than 8 million euros get a bit more time and only have to start issuing and receiving invoices 24 months after the final publication of the Ministerial Order, and start exchanging corresponding statuses​ 36 months after that final publication. This phased approach aims to improve fiscal transparency and automation while giving organizations time to align their systems and processes. 

The mandate requires businesses to issue and receive domestic electronic B2B invoices compliant with the European standard EN-16931. To ensure a smooth transition, the regulations allow some flexibility in data formatting — supporting CII, UBL, EDIFACT, and Factura-e — as well as in connectivity, enabling suppliers and buyers to exchange data via private platforms, the official public platform or a hybrid combination. Reporting on payment status and acceptance/rejection must be sent to the public platform within 4 days. 

SAP Business Network will support the outbound flow, i.e. issuing invoices via SAP Business Network, as well as the inbound flow to allow buying customers to receive invoices from both the network and external sources. 

In other words,  suppliers on SAP Business Network can keep sending invoices without major changes,  and keep benefiting from the Network value while being fully compliant with the Spanish mandate. With less manual intervention, suppliers are reducing errors and exceptions and allow for business rules validation and faster processing. This leads to quicker approvals and earlier payments.​ 

To support your readiness, we organize two webcasts in the near future on the Spanish e-invoicing mandate and related SAP Business Network support: 

  • English session: July 8, 2026 at 4:00 PM CEST – Register here 
  • Spanish session: July 8, 2026 at 10:00 AM CEST – Register here 

We are looking forward to welcoming you in these short conferences, which will remain available as recordings after the event.

For additional information on e-invoicing compliance in different countries, please visit our SAP Business Network for Suppliers page.

entry.title: Re: Extending Form Templates for Sales Document Output
entry.content:
Great blog post! I'd like to add information about another enhancement that became available in release 2602.2 — the ability to use calculated fields in custom form templates for sales document output.

With this new capability, key users can now define calculated header or item fields (along with their BAdI implementations) for sales order output. When generating the output, the system calculates the values of these fields at runtime based on the respective BAdI logic. This effectively extends the Form Data Provider (FDP) service for order confirmation (FDP_V1_ORDER_CONFIRM_SRV).

Typical Use Cases:

Here are some realistic business scenarios where calculated fields shine:

  1. Dynamic Delivery Lead Time — Calculate and print the expected delivery lead time on the order confirmation based on the requested delivery date, plant calendar, and current workload — giving customers a reliable commitment date.
  2. Customer Tier / Loyalty Status Label — Derive a customer's loyalty tier (e.g., "Gold Partner", "Preferred Customer") from their purchase history or customer classification, and print it prominently on the confirmation for a personalized touch.
  3. Computed Total Net Weight or Volume — Aggregate net weight or total volume across all line items at runtime, useful when the standard output form does not carry a pre-calculated total, or when custom UoM conversions are involved.
  4. Payment Terms Description — Combine payment term code, cash discount percentage, and due date logic into a human-readable description (e.g., "2% 10 days, net 30") derived dynamically from multiple fields and conditions.

How it works:

  1. In the Custom Fields app, create a data source extension for the Sales: Sales Order Confirmation Form data source.
  2. On the Calculated Fields tab, create your calculated fields — header-level fields for SalesOrderHeaderExtensionType, and item-level fields for SalesOrderItemExtensionType.
  3. Enter a description for the corresponding BAdI implementation, then save and publish the extension.
  4. Open the BAdI implementation and add custom logic — the header-level BAdI is SD_SLS_FDP_ORDR_HDR_CALC and the item-level BAdI is SD_SLS_FDP_ORDR_ITM_CALC.

In your custom logic, you can determine the field values by applying hardcoded values, retrieving data from CDS views, or calling classes/methods — making this a very flexible mechanism.

Example BAdI Implementation (SD_SLS_FDP_ORDR_ITM_CALC):

Below is a short pseudo-code snippet illustrating how you might implement the item-level BAdI to calculate a "Confirmed Delivery Days" field:

METHOD if_sd_sls_fdp_ordr_itm_calc~calculate.
  LOOP AT it_sales_order_item INTO DATA(item).
    " Calculate delivery days from today to requested delivery date
    DATA(delivery_date) = item-RequestedDeliveryDate.
    DATA(today)         = cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( ).
    DATA(delivery_days) = delivery_date - today.

    " Populate the calculated extension field
    DATA(extension) = VALUE salesorderitemextensiontype(
      salesorderitem            = item-SalesOrderItem
      yy1_confirmeddelivdays_it = |{ delivery_days }| ).

    INSERT extension INTO TABLE ct_sales_order_item_extension.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

This pattern — reading standard item data, applying business logic, and writing results into the extension type — applies equally to header-level calculations via SD_SLS_FDP_ORDR_HDR_CALC.

Note: The BAdIs only appear in the Custom Logic app after the relevant data source extension has been published in the Custom Fields app.

For more details, see: How to Extend a Custom Form Template with Calculated Fields

entry.title: @sap/cds-rfc: RFC_COMMUNICATION_FAILURE (NIECONN_BROKEN) in BAS using cds bind --exec, but works aft
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I am trying to run a CAP Node.js application locally in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) using hybrid mode (cds bind --exec). The application connects to an on-premise SAP system via Destination Service + Connectivity Service + Cloud Connector using @SAP/cds-rfc.

The exact same application works perfectly when deployed to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, but fails when run locally in BAS.

Environment

  • SAP Business Application Studio

  • Node.js: 22.13.1

  • @SAP/cds: 9.9.1

  • @SAP/cds-dk: 9.9.1

  • @SAP/cds-rfc: 2.2.1

  • @SAP-rfc/node-rfc-library: 1.1.2

  • @SAP-cloud-sdk/connectivity: 4.7.0

Hybrid setup

Service bindings are created successfully:

cds bind --to navisaccess-auth
cds bind --to navisaccess-connectivity
cds bind --to navisaccess-destination

Running:

cds bind --exec -- node rfctest.js

correctly injects all Cloud Foundry service bindings.

Minimal reproducible example

const cds = require("@sap/cds");

(async () => {
    try {
        console.log("Connecting...");

        const client = await cds.connect.to("P23_ABAP", {
            credentials: {
                destination: "P23_ABAP"
            }
        });

        console.log("Connected.");

        console.log("Calling RFC_PING...");

        const result = await client.send("RFC_PING", {});

        console.log(result);
    } catch (err) {
        console.error(err);
    }
})();

Output

Connecting...
Connected.
Calling RFC_PING...

RFCException NIECONN_BROKEN :
unable to receive data from connectivityproxy.internal.cf.ap10.hana.ondemand.com:20001

RFC_COMMUNICATION_FAILURE

Stack trace points to:

RFCService.begin()
createBapiClientConnectionHandler()
RFCClientAddonConnectionHandler.open()

What I have verified

  • cds bind works successfully.

  • Destination Service binding is successful.

  • Connectivity Service binding is successful.

  • XSUAA binding is successful.

  • cds.connect.to() succeeds.

  • The destination is resolved successfully.

  • The RFC client is created successfully.

  • The failure occurs only when the first RFC call (RFC_PING or GET_SYSTEM_TIME_REMOTE) is executed.

  • The exact same code works after deploying the application to Cloud Foundry using cf push.

  • It has has never been tested locally in BAS.

My question

Is RFC access through @SAP/cds-rfc + SAP Connectivity Service + Cloud Connector officially supported when running locally using cds bind --exec (hybrid mode)?

If yes, is there any additional configuration required for BAS or hybrid execution?

Or is RFC communication through Cloud Connector only supported for applications running inside the Cloud Foundry runtime?

Any guidance or documentation would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: Re: Managing Invoices with SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (now SAP Ariba Invoicing)
entry.content:
Hello @Abhijit_Shinde,

We are implementing SAP CIM (Ariba Invoicing) for our customer and they want to use only manual, email and peppol via SAP DRC as inbound channel. 
Countries in scope are: Germany (XRechnung requirement) and Sweden (PEPPOL requirement).

1. Does SAP Business Network comes together with SAP Ariba Invoicing (CIM) or does it need separate license ? If no, PO needs to be sent from S/4 Hana public cloud to SBN, this would be commerce automation. would be extra cost for this setup ?
From our perspective SBN would be best option to create and submit Xrechnung for germany. 

2. If supplier creates X rechnung from SBN, do we still need SAP DRC to receive and fetch invoice in CIM ?

3. Does receiving invoicing from PEPPOL Network (via DRC) to CIM still need SBN Integration ? If yes, what will be the flow ? If using PEPPOL network, do suppliers need onboarding on PEPPOL just like SBN?

4. If supplier sends XML (Xrechnung) via email, do we need SAP DRC to extract that data into CIM? If yes, how it can be fetched, because in this case we need SAP DRC and RPA both.

5. Our customer has entilements for sap ariba invoicing and sap ariba invoicing inbound processing module but they are yet to provision it. will be provisioned new way (sap managed) or old way (customer managed). How to know for which scope entitlements are (4N6 or 7YH)?

 

Thanks for your response.

entry.title: Unable to select transaction from transaction history dropdown in WEB GUI
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

We are experiencing an issue with the transaction history dropdown in SAP GUI.

When clicking on the transaction field (e.g. entering SM51) and opening the list of recently used transactions, the entries are displayed correctly. However, clicking directly on the transaction name does not select it.

The transaction can only be selected by clicking on the far left edge of the list item. Clicking on the text or anywhere else within the row has no effect.

This behavior makes the transaction history difficult to use and appears to indicate that the clickable area is misaligned with the displayed text.

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 19 - CDS source formatter
Function module RSDMD_API_DELETE does not delete
entry.content:
Hello BI fans,

we use FM RSDMD_API_DELETE in a custom webdynpro to delete a single value of an infoobject. Of course the value is not used in any info provider (no transaction data).

The RC is always 0, but the value is not deleted.

CALL FUNCTION 'RSDMD_API_DELETE'
            EXPORTING
              i_iobjnm               = ‘HH_XX’

              i_t_data                 = lt_attr_neu

              i_flg_delete_all     = rs_c_true

              i_flg_delete_texts = rs_c_false.

LT_ATTR_NEU[1] (in the debugger)

RECORD_NO                                              1

IOBJNM                                            HH_XX

VALUE                                             value

A) If HH_XX is a compounded infoobject the VALUE field is concatenated (1st compound field, 2nd compound field, value).

-> RC is always 0, but nothing is deleted

B) If HH_XX is a non compounded infoobject the VALUE field is only the value.

-> RC is always 0, but nothing is deleted.

I have also tried with COMMIT WORK after CALL FUNCTION and RC=0, but it makes no difference.

And the worst thing: when I pass a non-existent value to the FM the RC is 0 too! Crazy!

Unfortunately the FM has no exceptions and the documentation doesn’t explain the meaning of the export parameters.

The 4 export parameters in the debugger of the FM RSDMD_API_DELETE (before ENDFUNCTION):

L_RESULT                                       6

L_T_SIDVAL_USED                        [0x2(12)]Initial Standard Table

E_COUNT_USE                               0

L_T_PROTOCO                                [0x13(654)]Initial Standard Table

The included FM RSDMD_DEL_MASTER_DATA explains the export parameter e_result in his documentation (these should be constants):

RSDMD_C_ALL_DELETED                      0

RSDMD_C_PARTS_DELETED                  1

RSDMD_C_BG_STARTED                         2

RSDMD_C_NONE_DELETED                  4

RSDMD_C_NONE_DELETEABLE          

RSDMD_C_ERROR                                    5

But what means the return value 6?

If I don't pass the paramter i_flg_delete_texts L_RESULT=0, but nothin has been deleted.

 

Does anyone have experience using this FM? I don’t find a note according this problem.

The release is SAP BW/4HANA 2023, DW4CORE 4000006

 

Thanks and best wishes, Britta

entry.title: Re: Behind the Curtains - what's new about Maintenance Certificates
entry.content:
Hi SAP,

my question goes in the direction of 1jules question and, apologies, I don't understand your answer.

We are struggling in setup of maintenance certificate check in combination with Cloud ALM.

We have configured Cloud ALM option for additional Hana metrics (https://support.sap.com/en/alm/sap-cloud-alm/operations/expert-portal/setup-managed-services/calm-setup-hana.html)

Federation Lead is always saphostagent of HANA DB
Federation peer are always the SAP application server of the HANA DB

Cloud ALM monitoring is working by that.

Now we want to configure maintenance certificate check, like described here.

We have configured SAP Support Backbone Proxy on a central saphostagent where saprouter SRO is running (sap router for SAP to access the systems). Connection to SAP is working. We could already added saphostagents to the federated network and maintenance certificate checks in SAP systems works. So for this configuration the setup is:

Federation Lead is always saphostagent on SRO
Federation peer are always the SAP application server

When we now want to add SAP application server to SRO where we have already configured Cloud ALM we got error message:

Peer [FQDN hostname SAP AppServer] is already part of another SAP Host Agent Federation [Lead CA: 'CN=FQDN hostname HANA DB, OU=SAPHostAgent.INTCOM, OU=SAP Host Agent Certificate Authority, O=SAP Host Agent, C=DE'].

So a federation peer can only be connected to one federation lead.

Question is, in this scenario where we want to use Cloud ALM and maintenance certificate checks via federated network what would be SAP recommendation for set up, especially in terms of who should be federation lead and who federation peer?
Would the setup one federation lead (SRO) and all other saphostagents (SAP application server, HANA DB) are federation peers connects to SRO works for Cloud ALM as well?

Best regards,
Dennis

entry.title: Re: "System temporarily unavailable" for certification C_CPI_2601
entry.content: I've encountered the same "System temporarily unavailable" error on multiple scheduled attempts for C_CPI_2601. On the occasion I was able to access the exam, I observed a separate issue in Task 2: the on-premise API Provider connection test returned HTTP 503, and the publish-to-Developer-Hub step was not available. In my case the affected attempt was regranted automatically, so no attempt appears to have been deducted. I've raised support cases covering both the access error and the in-exam environment issue: 5000353119 — Issue accessing C_CPI_2601 exam environment (raised 2026-05-25) 5000354127 — Repeated issue accessing exam environment (raised 2026-05-27) 5000372265 — Task 2 cannot be completed: provider 503 + Developer Hub not provisioned (raised 2026-06-22, in process) Responses so far have referenced maintenance and known issues being addressed, but I haven't yet received a confirmed resolution. I'll share an update here if support provides further detail. It may be helpful for others seeing the same behaviour to note their dates so the pattern is easier to track.

entry.title: Can we anywhere see when SAP change the rules in SAP Docuent AI?
entry.content:
Can we see if and when SAP changed the LLM or other rules in the OCR part of SAP Document AI.

We use SAP Document AI via API calls and today we have had a number of requests that timed out. When we look in the UI, the documents are "DONE", but the API call did not answer within 1 minute. The response is usually much faster and we have never seen this problem before. Also when we re-load the documents the response time is about 10-20 seconds. 

The reason I suspect some updates is that after some time, the timeout stopped, but now the data is suddenly OCR extracted in an other way. Before we had an line item OCR error - we had same error since we starte the project a few months ago. Now this error  is solved and instead we see a new error.
Best Regards

Thomas Madsen Nielsen

Hi Team, 

There is an interface SOAP synch from source. while sending the response back , BTP automatically adding the SOAP env as below

But source system is expecting the tag as 

<soap-env:Envelope xmlns:soapenv="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">

Can you please let me know what is the solution for this. Please note the source adapter has to be SOAP synch

entry.title: Using SSO with Hana 2.0 and Azure AD
entry.content:
We have SAP Hana DB 2.00.079 and i want to login XS Classic engine with Azure AD. I created a Azure test account, configure single sign-on with SAML and everything working as expected.

I changed Hana side config/certificates for Azure prod but I couldn't login with prod Azure account. I enabled the traces and I can see below error.
Authentication SAMLAuthenticator.cpp(01122) : %%% [error: 4040007] [correlation id: 0x9e9a3ab65af736fe] Internal error occurred: Invalid principal id for principal .
XSSession XSSessionLifecycle.cpp(00379) : Assertion authentication for user xxx@xxx.com failed with reason: ptime::PtimeException

I am login Azure test account with user/pass. Prod Azure account working with windows session, so it is using X509 login to Azure.

I checked both Prod and Test Azure SAML Responses, key differences like below.

ProdTest
<Subject>
<NameID Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress">xxx@xxx.com</NameID>
<Subject>
<NameID Format="urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:unspecified">xxx@xxx.com</NameID>

<AttributeStatement>

<Attribute Name="http://schemas.microsoft.com/claims/authnmethodsreferences">
<AttributeValue>http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/authenticationmethod/x509</AttributeValue>
<AttributeValue>http://schemas.microsoft.com/claims/multipleauthn</AttributeValue>
</Attribute>

<AttributeStatement>

<Attribute Name="http://schemas.microsoft.com/claims/authnmethodsreferences">
<AttributeValue>http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/authenticationmethod/password</AttributeValue>
<AttributeValue>http://schemas.microsoft.com/claims/multipleauthn</AttributeValue>
<AttributeValue>http://schemas.microsoft.com/ws/2008/06/identity/authenticationmethod/unspecified</AttributeValue>
</Attribute>
<AuthnStatement AuthnInstant="2026-06-23T06:58:06.566Z" SessionIndex="_ef3f88b7-4876-40c6-b948-0c838ab31c00">
<AuthnContext>
<AuthnContextClassRef>urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:Unspecified</AuthnContextClassRef>
</AuthnContext>
</AuthnStatement>
<AuthnStatement AuthnInstant="2026-06-19T08:47:16.019Z" SessionIndex="_b98a9868-70a8-4137-a071-eef985d76100">
<AuthnContext>
<AuthnContextClassRef>urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:2.0:ac:classes:Password</AuthnContextClassRef>
</AuthnContext>
</AuthnStatement>

Hana can read to user NameID from SAML Response but i couldn't match the user. I hope someone can share some clue.

entry.title: Re: Where CIT sent from Network? Either to SAP Convergent...
entry.content:
Hi Gary,

Yes, CIT's (Consumption ITems) are description of unrated usages.

The standard process goes as follows:

- When getting usage requests, your mediation system sends chargeable items to Convergent Charging in a process called acquisition.
- CC then converts those chargeable items, batches them into CSV files, and sends them all to CI.
- CI converts the records to Consumption ITems (similar data, enhanced again), and keeps them in a module called Data Storage.
- Later, you pick up the CIT's from Convergent Invoicing's Data Storage, and send them back to CC, in a process called charging.
- This is when CC actually runs your pricing logic against the unrated usage descriptions, and this produces charged items (also batched by CC in CSV files).
- CC then sends the charged items to Convergent Invoicing, and this generates Billable Items (similar to charged items, but converted by CI for invoicing purposes, this time).

From Convergent Charging's point of view, these topics are addressed in several sections of the Application help guide:

  • transactional data (brief definitions: billable item, consumption item, etc)
  • chargeable items acquisition process
  • chargeable items charging process
  • acquisition using the Core Server (detailed textual explanation)
  • deferred charging (with a flowchart describing the whole process)

Feel free to let us know if you need further guidance.
Best regards.

François
SAP Convergent Charging Support

entry.title: From Strategy Doc to Runtime Rule: Embedding SAP AI Standards into Claude Code
entry.content:
What if your AI coding assistant already knew every relevant product standard before you typed a single line?

Disclaimer

This is a personal exploration, not an official SAP tool or recommendation. It is grounded in SAP's published standards and shaped by real development work, but the ANAR framework, the Claude Code integration, the hooks, and the skills described here are not SAP products and are not an endorsed approach. This is an experiment in what becomes possible when published strategy and engineering standards are treated as machine-readable development instructions rather than as PDFs on a SharePoint. Where I refer to SAP standards or tool availability, readers should verify applicability through their own official SAP channels before using anything like this in a productive context.

 

The Gap Between Strategy and Code

SAP has done serious work defining what "AI-native" can mean for BTP products. There is the AI-Native North Star Architecture, the AI Golden Path, and a growing body of product standards and development guidelines that together describe many of the requirements for a production-grade AI-native BTP application.

The problem I kept running into: these documents live in different repositories. My code lives in a CAP project. There is a wide gap between reading a standard and having it influence the code I am actually writing on a Tuesday afternoon.

The usual answer to this gap is a review checklist: someone runs an assessment at the end of a sprint or before a release, flags what is missing, and the team scrambles to retrofit it. I have done this. It is slow, expensive, and it catches things too late.

I have been calling this assessment lens ANAR - AI-Nativeness and Agent-Readiness. It is a framework I have been developing, based on SAP's official product standards and north star architecture, for assessing how ready a BTP product actually is for AI-native operation. It has ten weighted domains: Strategic Vision, UX & Joule, Process & Agents, Data & Grounding, API & Discoverability, Agent Tooling, AI Runtime, Security & Governance, Platform & Ops, and Commercialization. It also has twelve hard gates that cap the maximum score if they fail.

The maturity bands:

ScoreBand
85-100AI-Native
70-84Agent-Ready
55-69AI-Ready
35-54Pilot
0-34Not AI-Ready

The point of the bands is to make the next step concrete. A product at Pilot band might have two specific things blocking AI-Ready: G4, no direct LLM imports, and G8, data protection. Those are two engineering tasks, not a vague "improve your AI maturity".

But the more interesting question is not how to score your product. It is how to stop needing the score as a retrofit:

What if the standards were not something you checked against, but something that was already part of the environment you code in?

 

The Idea: Standards as Constitution

Claude Code - an AI coding assistant available in my development context - supports project memory through CLAUDE.md. It is a Markdown file that sits at the root of your project and is loaded as persistent project context. Whatever you put in it can strongly shape the assistant's behaviour, but it is not a policy engine. For hard enforcement, I used hooks.

That was the insight. A product standard regarding AI agent security might haves a set of requirements: objective declaration, constraints, tool permissions, Human-in-the-loop (HITL) policy, emergency stop endpoint. Those are not only audit criteria. They can also be expressed as coding rules. Some rules belong in the assistant's project context. The nonnegotiables need enforcement.

So I wrote CLAUDE.md as an ANAR constitution: twelve standards-aware sections, each mapping directly to a SAP product standard, written as coding rules rather than compliance prose.

## Audit Logging — G10 
Every state-changing handler MUST call audit.log() with:
  actor_type ('agent'|'human'), actor_id, object_type, object_id,
  tenant, x-correlation-id.
console.log() is NOT a substitute.

Not "please consider logging". A standing rule in the project context. The constitution gives the assistant the rules; the hooks make the nonnegotiables enforceable.

 

The Framework: Four Layers

Layer 1 - Foundation (always-on)

CLAUDE.md keeps the core rules visible in every session and gives the assistant the context it needs to generate standards-aware code. Alongside it, a set of memory files load the reference knowledge the assistant needs: all 12 gate definitions, canonical code patterns per gate, 15 anti-patterns with grep signatures, and a SAP service to ANAR domain map.

Layer 2 - Interception (real-time)

Claude Code supports hooks - shell scripts that fire before and after tool calls. This is where guidance becomes guardrail. I wrote four:

Hook

Trigger

What it does

gate-guard.js

Before every Write/Edit

Blocks direct LLM imports (G4), warns on unprotected CDS actions (G9), warns on missing audit.log() (G10)

anar-lint.sh

After every Write/Edit

Dispatches file-type-specific lint: CDS files check @restrict and ORD annotations; agent YAML checks security fields; mta.yaml checks service bindings

deploy-gate.js

Before any deploy command

Reads the ANAR scorecard and blocks deployment according to the configured gate policy

session-delta.sh

End of session

Shows ANAR-relevant changes, current score, and the highest-value next action

 

The gate-guard hook is the one that changed my workflow most noticeably. The first time it blocked a require('xyzai') import with:

[ANAR/G4] Direct LLM import - use permitted ai-api

I realised what ambient standards feedback actually feels like.

Layer 3 - Skills (on-demand)

Claude Code skills can be invoked directly with slash commands and work like specialized instructions the assistant executes on request. I built twelve. Here are the most useful:

  • /anar-scaffold <Entity> - generates a complete ANAR-compliant CAP service: schema.cds with @ODM.entityName and @PersonalData annotations, service.cds with @restrict on every action, service.js with audit logging and actor_type detection, plus mta.yaml additions
  • /anar-agent <AgentName> - scaffolds a code-based agent with all seven safeguards in the definition YAML
  • /anar-genai-call <UseCase> - generates the correct SAP AI Core SDK call pattern, never a direct third-party import
  • /anar-gate [G1-G12] - checks a specific gate against the current codebase with evidence and exact remediation steps
  • /anar-review - full 10-domain assessment, scores 0-5 per domain, updates the scorecard JSON, reports the delta
  • /anar-deploy-check [env] - pre-deployment checklist that distinguishes dev, staging, and prod strictness

Layer 4 - Intelligence (agents + MCP)

Two agent definitions can run as autonomous subagents: a Gate Guardian that monitors gate compliance and posts structured findings, and an ANAR Coach that explains requirements, generates examples, and runs gap analyses. Plus an MCP server exposes five tools for live standard lookups, callable by other agents in multi-agent workflows.

 

The Lifecycle in Practice

The way this plays out in a session:

While coding: CLAUDE.md means scaffolded output tends to include @ODM.entityName, @restrict, audit.log(), and actor_type detection from the start. The gate-guard hook catches anything important that slips through.

End of sprint: /anar-review scores the current state, updates the scorecard, and tells you the three highest-value things to do next, ranked by domain weight multiplied by score gap. Not a generic "improve security", but something like "Add emergency_stop_endpoint to order-agent/definition.yaml (G7, +4 pts)."

Before deployment: deploy-gate.js fires automatically and applies the configured gate policy. In my framework, prod mode requires all twelve gates to pass. Staging blocks on the six critical security gates. Dev mode is advisory only.

Before commerzialisation: /anar-commercialize walks through the commercialization checklist, checks for feature_id in agent definitions, and verifies metering hooks are wired. /anar-release-gate ai-native produces a local readiness statement that can be attached to a release PR as supporting evidence, not as a replacement for official governance.

The anar-scorecard.json is the living state: committed alongside the code, updated by /anar-review after each sprint, with a sprint log of score progression over time. It is the audit trail.

 

What This Is Not

This is not a product. It is not a supported SAP tool. It is not even a finished framework.

It is an experiment in a specific hypothesis: that the gap between "we have good standards" and "our code follows them" is primarily a tooling gap, not a knowledge gap. Engineers generally know what they should do. The question is whether the default path - the path of least resistance in the IDE - produces standards-aware code or not.

CLAUDE.md + hooks makes the standards-aware path the path of least resistance. That is the only claim I am actually making.

It also does not replace official review, approval, security, data-protection, or release-governance processes. At best, it moves many obvious issues earlier, where they are cheaper to fix.

The ANAR framework itself - the 10 domains, the 12 gates, the scoring - is my interpretation of SAP's published standards. I have tried to be rigorous about grounding every rule in a specific product standard, but interpretations can be wrong, standards evolve, and my coverage is certainly incomplete. If you are doing this for a real product heading toward production, run the actual SAP AI self-assessment process. Use the official tools. This is for experimenting, learning, and building intuition about what AI-native BTP development actually requires.

 

Building Your Own

The pattern does not require any specific repo. For a real SAP product, I would treat this as a local developer-experience layer, not as a replacement for official assessments, architecture reviews, security review, data-protection review, or release governance.

The minimal version is three things.

1. A project memory file that translates standards into rules.

Pick the product standards that apply to your product. For each one, write a concrete coding rule - not "ensure authorization is applied" but "every CDS action and function must have @restrict or @requires. No exceptions." The more specific the rule, the more reliably the assistant can follow it.

2. A PreToolUse hook that enforces the non-negotiables.

One Node.js script, called before every file write, can grep-match for the patterns you absolutely cannot ship: direct LLM provider imports, unprotected CDS actions, or state-change handlers without an audit call. Anything that should be a hard block goes here.

3. A scorecard JSON committed to the repo.

One file records the current domain scores, gate statuses, and sprint log. It does not need to be automated at first. Even a manually updated JSON makes the current state visible, trackable over time, and readable by any agent or script that needs to reason about it.

Those three pieces substantially narrow the gap between "we have a standard" and "our code follows it" - not by eliminating judgment, but by making the standards-aware path the path of least resistance. Everything else - the skills, the agents, the MCP server - builds on top of that foundation.

The authoritative sources to ground your own rules in:

  • SAP AI-Native North Star Architecture - domain definitions and maturity model
  • SAP's AI Golden Path - approved technology stack and agent patterns
  • SAP BTP Developer's Guide - platform conventions and approved libraries

These are official SAP resources. Ask your product standard owner or check your own internal developer portal.

 

What Is Next

The experiment I am running next: making the scorecard itself an agent-readable artifact, so a planning agent can read the current band and open backlog, then generate a prioritized engineering plan to reach the next one. If that works, the gap between assessment and sprint planning closes too.

I would be interested to hear how others are turning architecture standards, security requirements, or AI governance rules into developer-time feedback loops rather than end-of-sprint review checklists.

entry.title: Choosing the Right Component When Opening a Support Case for SAP GTS Edition for Hana
entry.content:
If you're using SAP Global Trade Services Edition for HANA 2023 or higher and need to open a support case through the SAP for Me GetSupport app, selecting the correct component is crucial for efficient issue resolution. This guide will help you navigate the component selection process and get your case routed to the right team quickly.

Why Component Selection Matters

When you open a case in SAP Get Support app, the product and component you select determines which support team reviews your issue. Choosing the correct component ensures your case reaches the right experts faster, reducing resolution time and improving your overall support experience.

Component Naming Convention Change

If you've been working with SAP Global Trade Services for a while, you may be familiar with the legacy SLL-LEG* component. This component is still valid and is used for lower releases of Global Trade Services and integration with ERP and GTS products

However, starting with SAP Global Trade Services Edition for HANA 2023, the correct component prefix has changed to FT-LEG.*
If you're on the 2023 release or higher, please ensure you're using the new FT-LEG* components outlined in this guide.

The Main Component: FT-LEG*

For all issues related to SAP Global Trade Services Edition for HANA 2023 and higher, you'll be working within the FT-LEG* component family. This parent component encompasses the various functional areas of Global Trade Services.

Selecting Your Specific Component

Depending on the area where you're experiencing an issue, select the appropriate sub-component:

Core Functional Process Areas

  • Customs Management Processes: FT-LEG-CUS*
  • Compliance Management Processes: FT-LEG-CON*
  • Preference Management Processes: FT-LEG-PRE*
  • Intrastat Process: FT-ITR-ISR
  • Classification Content Process: FT-LEG-MDT-CCT
  • Product Classification: FT-LEG-MDT-CCT
  • Master Data: FT-LEG-MDT*

Integration Issues with SAP S/4HANA

If your issue involves integration with your SAP S/4HANA feeder system, select from these integration-specific components:

  • Compliance Management Integration: FT-INT-GTS-CON
  • Customs Management Integration: FT-INT-GTS-CUS
  • Preference Management Integration: FT-INT-GTS-PRE
  • Business Partner Transfer: FT-INT-GTS-CBP
  • Product Master Transfer: FT-INT-GTS-CPM

How to Choose

Ask yourself these questions:

  1. What process area is affected? (Customs, Compliance, Preference, Intrastat, or Classification)
  2. Is the issue within GTS itself, or is it related to data transfer from S/4HANA?
    • If it's within GTS: Choose the appropriate FT-LEG* component
    • If it involves integration: Choose the corresponding FT-INT-GTS* component

Next Steps

Once you've identified the correct product and component, open your case in SAP for Me GetSupport with confidence. Providing this information upfront will help support specialists understand your issue more quickly and work toward a resolution and also provide more self help options specific to your product.

Need help deciding? Review your issue details against the component descriptions above, the app can also help to drill down more to the specific subcomponent for your specific issue, once the main component has been determined..
When in doubt, it's better to provide additional context in your case description—our support teams can always reassign if needed.

entry.title: SAP Datasphere: How to create or reuse Replication Flows and Local table with 2 Source System ?
entry.content:
Hi Gurus,

A client with multiple companies currently has SAP S4HANA PRD and SAP Datasphere PRD in production, both containing data from Company 1.

They also have S4HANA QAS1 and SAP Datasphere QAS1 in quality assurance. Now, SAP Datasphere QAS1 needs to extract data from a new S4HANA QAS2 repository belonging to Company 2.

Should I create new replication flows and local tables, or use the existing local tables and only create the replication flow? 

My main concern is the transportation of local tables and replication flows that already exist in production.

 

Regards.

entry.title: New Accelerated Depreciation Calculation
entry.content:
Hi All,

We need New Depreciation Method to be Created  in a way that it should calculate Depreciation for the 1st Year(12 Months from Asset Acquisition Month) - 65% of Asset Value and from 2nd Year (13 the Month of the Asset) Onwards Depreciation should be calculated equally for remaining life of the asset on the NBV of the asset after 1st Year.

 

Below is the example, if Asset Acquired in Jul 2026 at 36000 and Useful life 3 Years.

Depreciation will be Calculated at 65% from Jul 2026 to Jun 2027 (65% of 36000) = 23400

 From Jul 2027 it should be SLM from NBV after Jun 2027 - (24300/2 Years) = 6300.

Below is the Tabular way how it will Show the Depreciation per year.

  Acqu ValueDeprnNBV 
<2026    RON
202636000360001170024300RON
2027 36000148509450RON
2028 3600063003150RON
2029 3600031500RON
 36000 36000 RON

 

We are able to achieve this if the Asset is Acquired in January. But if the Asset is Acquired in Middle of any year(like Above) the 65% Calculation is Restricting only for the year 2026. and from 2027 its Calculating at SLM from NBV after Dec 2026, Which is Not Correct.

 

If anyone of you have Implemented such kind of Depreciation Could you please help with the Solution.

 

Thank You in Advance

entry.title: Advanced Scripting in SAC: How to use Page Navigation & Popup Panels
entry.content:
There is a moment when a SAC story stops feeling like a report and starts feeling like an application. For me, that moment came when I discovered scripting.

SAC's script editor lets you attach logic to user actions - a button click, a selection change, a page load. Once you understand even the basics, you can build navigation flows, show and hide panels, and guide users through your story the way a well-designed app would. The gap between a standard SAC dashboard and a polished, app-like experience is often just a handful of simple scripts.

In this post I will walk through the two patterns I use most: page navigation buttons and popup panels for drill-through detail.

 

1. What SAC Scripting Is (and Is Not)

SAC uses its own event-driven scripting language - not JavaScript, not Python. Scripts run in response to things the user does. The three events you will use most are:

  • onClick - triggered when a user presses a button. The workhorse of SAC scripting.
  • onChange - triggered when an input control changes, such as a dropdown selection.
  • onInitialization - triggered when a page loads. Useful for setting default states.

The trap I fell into early was assuming scripting was a developer's domain. In reality, the patterns used in 90% of scripted stories are copy-paste simple once you have seen them once. You do not need to memorise syntax - you need to recognise the pattern and adapt it to your widget names.

Key takeaway: Think of SAC scripting as reusable building blocks, not custom code from scratch.

                            

                            

 

2. Page Navigation with Buttons

The default SAC tab strip works, but it is not controllable - every page is always visible and you cannot guide users through a logical flow. With scripting, you replace it with buttons you design yourself.

The script is one line. On the onClick event of a button widget:

    Application.showPage("PageName");

Replace PageName with the internal name of your page and you have a fully functional navigation button. I recently used this to build a three-page story with a persistent navigation bar - Home, Trends, and Detail. Stakeholders asked whether it had been built in a different tool. It had not.

The one rule I would emphasise: consistency. Same button position, same size, same style on every page. The scripting is the easy part - keeping the navigation visually consistent is where the real craft lies.

Key takeaway: Custom navigation gives you full control over the user journey. Show only what is relevant at each step and make the story feel deliberate.

3. Popup Panels for Drill-Through Detail

Sometimes you want to show detail without sending users to a new page and losing their context. A popup panel - a container widget hidden by default and revealed on demand - solves this elegantly.

The pattern has four steps:

  • Create a container widget and fill it with your detail content - a chart, a table, or both.
  • In the container's onInitialization event, write: Container_1.setVisible(false);
  • Add a trigger button on the main canvas with onClick script: Container_1.setVisible(true);
  • Add a close button inside the container with onClick script: Container_1.setVisible(false);

The first time I used this in a live presentation, there was a visible shift in how the audience engaged. The conversation moved from 'what does this chart show?' to 'can we look at that region more closely?' - which is exactly the conversation you want.

Key takeaway: Popup panels let users go deeper without losing their place. It is the closest thing SAC has to a modal dialog, and one of the most effective patterns for making a story feel designed.

                   

                                  

 

 

4. Three Rules for Scripted Stories

Scripting introduces a new category of risk - things that work during development but confuse users when the story goes live. Three rules I now apply to every scripted build:

  • Always add a close or back button. If you open something with a script, close it with a script. This cost me a live demo where a stakeholder opened a popup and could not close it. The panel covered the entire canvas. Never again.
  • Keep scripts short. More than three or four lines usually means the story design needs simplifying. Each script should do one thing.
  • Test on a small screen. Popup panels sized on a large monitor can break entirely on a laptop. Always check on the smallest screen your audience is likely to use.

Key takeaway: Scripting discipline matters more than scripting skill. A simple, reliable story beats a clever, fragile one every time.

               

Final Thoughts

Scripting moved SAC from 'dashboard builder' to 'experience designer' in my mind. When you control the navigation and the interactions, you are not just presenting data - you are designing how someone engages with it.

I have covered most of the key topics to help beginners and beyond. If you have suggestions or topics you’d like to see, feel free to comment. I will keep updating as I learn more.

Start small - one button, one popup. Even 20 minutes can make your story feel more polished.

What scripting patterns have you tried in SAC? Share in the comments!

Thanks for reading, happy scripting! ✨

entry.title: Live vs. Import Models in SAP Analytics Cloud - How to Choose and Why It Matters
entry.content:
One of the first decisions you make when connecting data to SAC will affect everything that follows: do you use a Live model or an Import model? It shapes how your story performs, how fresh your data is, and how much control you have over the user experience.

I got this wrong on my first proper build - not catastrophically, but wrong enough that I had to rethink the approach partway through. The turning point came when users asked why the dashboard felt slow, and I realised the problem was not SAC.

In this post I will explain what each model type does, when each is the right choice, and how to avoid the trade-off trap I fell into.

                   

1. What Each Model Type Actually Does

A Live model connects SAC directly to the source system - SAP BW, HANA, or S/4HANA - and queries it in real time. Every filter you apply, every drill you execute, sends a query back to the source. The data you see is always current, because it has never left.

An Import model works differently. SAC pulls the data into its own in-memory storage on a schedule you define. The story runs against that snapshot. Data is current as of the last successful refresh — no earlier, no later.

The key difference is not just freshness , it is where computation happens. Live models push the heavy lifting to the source system. Import models handle it inside SAC's in-memory engine. This single distinction drives almost every trade-off between the two.

Key takeaway:  Live = always fresh, source does the work. Import = snapshot in time, SAC does the work.

                     

2. When to Use a Live Model

Live models are the right choice when data freshness is genuinely critical - financial closing dashboards, operational monitoring, real-time KPI tracking. If your stakeholders need to see data updating throughout the day without waiting for a refresh, Live is usually the answer. Use a Live model when:

  • Data freshness is measured in minutes, not hours
  • The source is SAP BW or HANA with well-optimised queries
  • Data volume is too large to import efficiently
  • Governance requires that data never leaves the source system

The thing I underestimated: the performance of a Live model depends entirely on the source system. If the HANA query behind your story is well-optimised, the dashboard feels instant. If it is not, every filter and drill feels slow - and there is nothing you can fix inside SAC. That is the lesson I learned the hard way on my first build.

Key takeaway:  Live models borrow their performance from the source. Know the health of your source before you commit.

                         

3. When to Use an Import Model

For the majority of analytical use cases - trend analysis, management reporting, self-service exploration; Import models deliver a noticeably better experience. Because the data sits in SAC's in-memory engine, filtering and drilling happen in milliseconds. Consider an Import model when:

  • Fast, interactive filtering and drilling is essential
  • Data volume is manageable - millions, not billions of rows
  • Your source is non-SAP (CSV, Excel, external APIs)
  • You need to blend data from multiple sources into one model
  • Your audience does self-service exploratory analysis

The trade-off is freshness. If your data updates hourly but your refresh schedule runs daily, the story will be a day behind. For most management reporting and trend analysis, this is entirely acceptable. For operational dashboards where someone is making a decision right now, it may not be.

Key takeaway:  Import models give you speed and flexibility  but you own the refresh schedule and its consequences.

                       

4. The Decision Framework (and the Hybrid Option)

After a few builds, I settled on a simple set of questions that almost always point to the right answer:

  • Is data freshness critical (minutes)? → Live
  • Is the source SAP BW or HANA with optimised queries? → Live viable
  • Do you need to blend sources in a single model? → Import
  • Is your audience doing self-service exploratory analysis? → Import
  • Unsure? → Start with Import, migrate to Live if freshness becomes an issue

The option that surprises people most is the hybrid approach. You can use a Live model for the headline KPI tiles that must always show current data, alongside an Import model for the trend charts where interaction speed matters more than real-time precision. Both can sit in the same SAC story, and your audience will rarely notice the difference — they will just notice that everything works.

Key takeaway:  The best choice matches your data's nature and your audience's needs — not the one that sounds most impressive.

Final Thoughts

The Live vs Import question is not a technical detail you can defer. It shapes every interaction your audience will have with your story such as how fast it feels, how fresh the data is, and how much flexibility you have to evolve it later.

My rule of thumb: start every new story by asking two questions. How often does my audience actually need this data to refresh? And where does my source system's query performance really live? The answers almost always point to the right model and sometimes they point to both.

What has your experience been with Live vs Import? I would love to hear what pushed you one way or the other, drop a comment below.

Thanks for reading - happy modelling!

entry.title: Re: Datasphere data flow null value in target table
entry.content: Hello Sravan, there is no format mismatch as both sides are labeled as dates. From the graphical view until the local table, the data type stays the same. There are no conversion functions anywhere. Also values are not null in the source so there is no null handling rule involved.

entry.title: Re: Document types supported in SSAM 2310
entry.content: SSAM 2310 does not support ZIP file upload/download natively. Only specific document types such as PDF, images (JPG, PNG), and common Office formats are supported.

entry.title: Department of Homeland E-Verify Account Certification: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
We're writing to inform you of an important update to E-Verify account management. Starting this year, E-Verify will require all users to validate and certify that their account information is current and correct. This change is designed to enhance security and ensure compliance with federal requirements.
 
Here is a link to the official announcement: New Requirement: E-Verify Account Validation | E-Verify 
 
You can act now: Authorized users can begin updating their contact information and Unique Entity Identifier (UEI) immediately by working with our Support team through a Support Case. Don't wait—get ahead of this requirement today.
 
What's Changing and When
Beginning this year, users must validate and certify their account information every 12 months. This is a one-time requirement per account—only one authorized user needs to complete it.
 
What happens if you don't certify on time? Account features, including case creation, will be blocked until certification is completed. We recommend prioritizing this to avoid service interruptions.

Federal Contractors: UEI Requirements

If your company is a federal contractor with a Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) E-Verify clause, this section applies to you.
 
Action required: Add your Unique Entity Identifier (UEI) number to the Company Information section of your Company Profile as soon as possible. The UEI is required to ensure proper identification and compliance with federal regulations.
 

How to Find Your UEI

Your UEI is issued through SAM.gov. If your company is registered in SAM.gov, it already has a UEI.
  1. Visit https://sam.gov/
  2. Log in with your company credentials
  3. Locate your UEI in your account information
  4. Contact our Support team to add it to your E-Verify Company Profile

No longer a federal contractor with FAR E-Verify requirements? We can also help update your company profile to reflect this change, and the UEI will no longer be required.

Get Started Today

Don't wait for the certification deadline!
Authorized users can work with our Support team right now to update contact information and UEI details through a Support Case. Contact Support to get your account information current and avoid any service interruptions.

Questions?

Our Support team is ready to help you navigate these changes. Whether you need assistance updating your company information, locating your UEI, or understanding the certification process, reach out to us through a Support Case.

We're committed to making this transition as smooth as possible for you and your team.

This update applies to all SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding E-Verify users. The phased rollout begins this year with additional communications to follow. Stay tuned for more details specific to your account.
